All right, you're back. Let's pick up a right where we left off with step number five, your customer onboarding and adoption strategies. Now, if you make that initial sale, a healthy onboarding experience is going to serve as the very foundation for long term success in terms of creating a customer for life. In essence, a promise was made to your new customer during the sale in terms of the actual value you plan to deliver. Right? Well, now you want to ensure that you meet your new customer's expectations. And again, one of the most effective ways to do that is through a well planned out onboarding process, which includes, among other things, an actual checklist for things like user provisioning, configuration, integration, and also most importantly, you have to consider customer training. You need to make sure that when they receive your product, good or service, that if there's a training program required, they have access to it. You get it? Now, during those first 90 days, either you or someone in your company's customer success team needs to track both usage and customer training and be prepared to quickly intervene if certain predetermined thresholds are not met. One of the most obvious things being you need to make sure that your customers are actually using your product. It also is like, hey, maybe tell you how common this problem is, especially with online purchase, things like exercise products, dieting regimens, learning products with a common thread here being the fact that the new customer has to actually exert some effort on their part to get the benefits they promised. You get it? In fact, in the absence of a direct intervention here, for me, yourself or someone in your company's customer success team, you'll find that as many as 20% of the people who bought your product will never even use it, despite having the best intentions when they actually made the purchase. In other words, you could have the most amazing product in the world, but if you don't engage in the first 90 days, then there's a 90% chance they never will. That's an actual statistic that's been studied. Now, as I said, sometimes people are just lazy. You don't correctly onboard because it's level of inertia in their own life. Now, in those cases, with just a little bit of a push in the form of a phone call or even a text or email or some sort of reward or incentive, and I'll get to those later on in a different lesson, it can go a very long way to rectifying things. In other cases, however, it can be something about the design of your product that confuses people or simply overwhelms them in terms of what they should do first, second, third, and so forth. And the existence of confusion or overwhelm is literally the death nail to a healthy onboarding process. So in those cases, you're going to attack that problem from two separate fronts. Number one, you're going to want to test out some new design changes. Now, if that's not in your power in terms of just a salesperson, I would bring that to the attention of the company, let them know what's going on. If they won't change it, well, it's their decision. You have to make sure you work around that by working twice as hard by calling up afterwards and helping them go through the onboarding. And that will offset that. And number two is another one, you want to call them personally either way and take them through the onboarding process. You get it? Now, as I said before, this intervention can be over the phone and it can also be in the form of some carefully worded emails or texts, albeit in those latter two cases, emails and texts, you're going to definitely want to embed a video in those texts or emails that takes them step by step through the onboarding process. And hence, offsets any feelings of confusion or overwhelm or just general inertia that currently is undermining the positive outcome. In other words, if no one's going to speak to them on the phone, then you don't want to have a very compelling video in there that not so gently prods them into taking action and make sure that it follows all the visual and audio protocols that govern the straight line system. You get it? Now, one very important point here, insofar as this classic commitment you're going to make to be a consistent under-promiser and over-deliver, you like that? Pents shift? Anyway, here's the deal. While you're always going to try your best to live up to that noble pursuit and it's noble, it's simply not always going to end up that way. You see, no matter how hard you try, there's going to be many occasions where even though you didn't actually over promise, you still ended up under delivery. In other words, there's going to be times when things that we go wrong in the sales process, there are going to be lots of unexpected problems, unforeseen challenges that pop up along the way they always do. And sometimes these things will be no fault of your own and frankly, other times you'll actually make a mistake, your company will make a mistake and that's okay. So here's the deal. Your customers will both accept and actually put up with this and be fine. See, they've been around the block long enough, they're not children, to know that things don't always go according to plan. What matters though, is that in your heart, you're always trying to do the right thing and that comes shining through to your new client with basically everything you do, every action you take and that includes a consistent effort to manage their current expectations. So when things go wrong, you want to quickly reset those expectations through what? Honest communication. You get it? Make sense? Okay, awesome. So let me sum things up here very quickly here. Number one, as part of your strategic preparation phase, you want to make it a point to do what? Under promise. And then step two, as part of your execution phase and commitment to excellence, you want to always do everything you're powered to what? Over deliver. Number three, and when things are not going according to plan, as they will, you want to put on your big boy or big girl pants, apologize on the spot to your new customer, tell them exactly what's happening, be honest and then do everything in your power to strain things out as quickly and as best as you can. See, in the end, it's all about effective problems solving. You want to be that regular breed of salesperson who takes any problem that might arise during the onboarding phase and you personally make good on it, meaning that even if you're not solving the problem yourself, you could be in the customer service department, right? You still want to be the one who follows up with them to tell them it's been resolved as opposed to saying, oh yeah, here's the customer service number. Good luck trying to get through. Call me in a month and you're still having problems. You get it? Rad, you want to be that guy or girl who sings things through to the very end personally and not only holds their hand every step of the way, but also you're glad to do it. You get that? And as it comes through, it's not a burden to you. It's actually something you look forward to, to hold their hand and to give them awesome customer service. That goes a really long way to creating a customer for life. See, in fact, there's a very big difference to you saying, I need to get back to you or I have to get back to you versus I'd be glad to get back to you. So that latter language pattern says to your customer is that rather than solving your problems as an obligation, I actually embrace that opportunity. And that goes the longest way of all towards painting a picture and you're going to be able to hold their hand every step of the way, as I said, and you're doing it gladly, not grudgingly. And again, people will always understand it's going to be problems, right? When they make purchases, that's what happens. But the worst thing you could do is to suddenly become absent when those problems arise. Right now, in general, sales pause, we refer to this as ducking a phone call. And it's pretty much the worst thing a sales person can do in terms of the long term relationship. In essence, when you duck a call means you're hiding behind your unanswered telephone or you're not returning your customer's texts or emails, because you're simply too embarrassed that the promises you made have been unfulfilled as of yet. Well, let me just tell you something. Once you duck a call, the relationship will never, ever be the same plate and simple. There's been a breach of trusting a duck a phone call and it's close to impossible to repair. You know, I said you lose the customer on the spot, but rather, you simply never get to that full point of trust where this person feels good about upsetting and giving you referrals, which should be the lifeblood of your business. You get it? In fact, it's strattin'. It's about the problems we had there and the mistakes I made personally. The one call rule we had was that you can never, ever duck a customer's phone call. Bottom line. What ends up happening there when you duck a call is the person getting angrier and angrier and angrier until they eventually cancel and probably file a formal complaint as well. In fact, now when I'm hired by a company to train the sales force, which is most of my business corporate training, right? When I'm training that sales force, the first policy I ask the manager to institute is that under no circumstances can a sales force and duck a phone call. And I'll keep saying this to you again and again throughout this module because ducking that call is the most destructive thing you can do when it comes to building a long-term relationship with your client. Bottom line. Again, as I said before, once you do it, it's never the same again. Okay? With that, let's stop and we'll take a quiz, go through some exercises, and I will see you very soon on the other side. Take care.
